coredata.dc:title: Bottom-up preparation of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots with green emission under microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment and their biological imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Lili
Li, Lin
Liu, Meiying
Wan, Qing
Tian, Jianwen
Huang, Qiang
Wen, Yuanqing
Liang, Shangdong
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have standout as one of the most important carbon nanomaterials owing to their small size, optical properties, biocompatibility and low toxicity. These CDs have been extensively explored for various applications especially in the biomedical fields. Although great efforts have been devoted to the fabrication of CDs through both top-down and bottom-up methods, most of these CDs exhibited blue emission under UV light irradiation. In this work, we reported a novel, green, economic approach for preparation of nitrogen doped CDs by directly microwave-assisted heating small organic molecules at 120°C for 5min using Glu and amino group of MPD as the precursors and ethylene glycol as solvent. Thus prepared CDs emit green fluorescence when they were irradiated under 365nm UV light. Compared with other synthetic methods, this microwave-assisted heating method does not require strict reaction conditions and is easy for operation. The CDs display unique luminescence properties, high water dispersibility and good biocompatibility. These remarkable features make CDs great potential for various biomedical applications. Taken together, a microwave-assisted heating method has been developed for bottom-up preparation of green emission CDs in a rather mild route. Owing to the facile experimental procedure and outstanding features of CDs, this work will open up a new avenue for fabrication and biomedical applications of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Green emission
Microwave-assisted heating
Bottom-up
Biological imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The optical properties of MPD-Glu CDs
Effects of pH and illumination on CDs
Cytotoxicity and cell imaging of MPD-Glu CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis illustration of preparation procedure of CDs by MPD and Glu through microwave irradiation assisted hydrothermal method.
(A) TEM image of MPD-Glu CDs, (B) size distribution of MPD-Glu CDs.
FT-IR spectrum of MPD-Glu CDs. A series of characteristic peaks indicated that MPD-Glu CDs were obtained from the hydrothermal treatment of MPD and Glu.
XPS survey spectrum of synthetic MPD-Glu CDs, (A) XPS full scan, (B) C 1s (C) N 1s (D) O 1s.
UV–Vis absorption spectrum of MPD-Glu CDs, inset show the photographs in water under 365nm UV lamp irradiation (left) and in day light (right).

(A) The excitation and emission spectra of spectra of MPD-Glu CDs. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are located at 370 and 500nm, respectively. (B) The excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of MPD-Glu CDs.
(A) The measurement of stability of MPD-Glu CDs. (B) The effect of pH on the MPD-Glu CDs.

(A) Cells viability of MPD-Glu CDs. L929 cells were incubated with different concentrations of MPD-Glu CDs for 24h. The cell viability was determined by MTT assay. (B–D) are the CLSM images of L929 cells, which were incubated with 30μgmL−1 of MPD-Glu CDs for 3h and then excited with 405nm laser. (B) Fluorescence image, (C) bright field, and (D) merged image of B and C.

head.ce:title: Bottom-up preparation of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots with green emission under microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment and their biological imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physiology, Medical School of Nanchang University, Nanchang 330006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of New Energy Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Provincial Key Laboratory of New Energy Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: X. Zhang, Department of Physiology, Medical School of Nanchang University, Nanchang 330006, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have standout as one of the most important carbon nanomaterials owing to their small size, optical properties, biocompatibility and low toxicity. These CDs have been extensively explored for various applications especially in the biomedical fields. Although great efforts have been devoted to the fabrication of CDs through both top-down and bottom-up methods, most of these CDs exhibited blue emission under UV light irradiation. In this work, we reported a novel, green, economic approach for preparation of nitrogen doped CDs by directly microwave-assisted heating small organic molecules at 120°C for 5min using Glu and amino group of MPD as the precursors and ethylene glycol as solvent. Thus prepared CDs emit green fluorescence when they were irradiated under 365nm UV light. Compared with other synthetic methods, this microwave-assisted heating method does not require strict reaction conditions and is easy for operation. The CDs display unique luminescence properties, high water dispersibility and good biocompatibility. These remarkable features make CDs great potential for various biomedical applications. Taken together, a microwave-assisted heating method has been developed for bottom-up preparation of green emission CDs in a rather mild route. Owing to the facile experimental procedure and outstanding features of CDs, this work will open up a new avenue for fabrication and biomedical applications of CDs.

A facile and fast microwave-assisted hydrothermal method has been developed for fabrication of carbon quantum dots with green emission.
Image 2

ce:section.ce:para: Many fluorescent materials contained semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) [1], metal fluorescent clusters [2], Ln ions doped materials [3,4], organic dyes [5], polymer dots [6–10] and carbon quantum dots [11–13] have been reported in the past few years, which have been widely used in bioimaging, biosensor, optoelectronic devices, detection of metal ions [14–17] due to their unique fluorescence properties. Especially, the fluorescent polymeric nanoparticles with aggregation-induced emission feature have recently attracted the most research attention owing to their enhanced luminescence intensity, well designability of structure and tenability of fluorescence properties and functions [18–23]. However, their practical applications have been shadowed because of their biological toxicity, poor water solubility, environmental pollution, complicated preparation process. Currently, carbon based fluorescent materials, which named carbon quantum dots (CDs), are discovered to be a newly class of fluorescent nanomaterials that have been attracted more and more attention for various applications. Compared to traditional fluorescent materials, CDs overcome the shortcomings, they have nontoxicity, water solubility, no optical blinking, environment friendliness, as well as good biocompatibility [24–26], which make CDs superior to traditional fluorescent materials to further increase potential application value in biomedical fields, such as drug carries, fluorescent probes [27], biological imaging [28–34], and disease therapy [35], etc.

Over the past few decades, many approaches have been developed to synthesis CDs with excellent characteristics by top-down methods, including chemical oxidation, hydrothermal, solvothermal routes, laser ablation, hydrazine hydrate reduction of graphene oxide, electrochemical oxidation [36–40], etc. Respectively, bottom-up methods involve pulsed laser-induced photochemical stitching (PLPS) method, ultrasonic treatment, thermal techniques [41–45]. Unfortunately, these methods of preparation CDs require cumbersome steps, strict restrictions like strong acids, high temperature, low quantum yield or environmental pollution and other shortcomings, which have limited their production in large scale. Furthermore, the synthetic CDs by previous works have short emission wavelengths (blue emission) and weak fluorescence intensity, the short emission wavelength usually need short excitation wavelengths, which are disadvantages for biomedical applications because of shorter excitation wavelengths with high energy has serious damage for the cell matrix and tissues [46]. On the other hand, weak fluorescence of CDs would influence the visualization if they were used as fluorescent agents for various biomedical applications [47,48]. Therefore, it is still highly desirable to develop a high-efficiency and environment friendly method to prepare CDs with better optical properties, excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity.

Comparing with previous methods, microwave-irradiation [49] has been regarded as a facile and high-efficiency tool to prompt the synthesis of CDs. It is time-saving, cost-effective, energy conservation and convenient operation [50]. Inspiring by these great advantages of microwave-assistant strategies, in this study, we reported for the first time a facile microwave-assistant method to prepare CDs with the raw materials of m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and d-(+)-glucose (Glu) in the ethylene glycol solution at 120°C for 5min. As is well known, Glu possess rich hydroxyl groups and it is therefore an ideal surface passivator to prepare CDs and endowing their great biocompatibility and enhancing hydrophilicity [51]. According to previous works, MPD contains independent electron pairs of amino functional groups, which could increase luminescent intensity of CDs when they attached to carbon framework [27,50,52]. Finally, compared with previous methods, the synthetic conditions of this method are rather mild (without high temperature, or strong acid). More importantly, the process is simple, economical, easy to control, and it takes only 5min to successfully prepare CDs with green emission. The prepared fluorescent CDs have ultra-small particle size, low toxicity, strong fluorescence, great water solubility and biocompatibility, which have been used to cell imaging in vitro. We expect this method could provide a new flat to produce CDs with excellent performances.

All reagents were of analytical grade and were used without any further purification. d-(+)-Glucose (Glu, Mw: 180.16Da, 99.5%), m-phenylenediamine (MPD, Mw: 108.14Da, 99%), ethylene glycol (Mw: 62.07Da, 98%), ethyl acetate (Mw: 88.11Da, 99.5%) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China) and were used directly as received. The MCR-3 microwave reactor was made in Zhengzhou Keda Machinery. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout this study. The microstructures of the as-prepared CDs were observed with a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEOL, JEM-2100F). Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were acquired using a PE-LS55 fluorescence spectrometer. The absorption spectra were performed by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Ruili Analytical instrument Company, Beijing, China). Both the fluorescence and UV absorption spectra were recorded with a fixed excitation wavelength of 360nm at room temperature. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed to verify the functional groups with a spectrum on a PE Spectrum One spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS; Thermo Fisher) measurements were performed by the ESCALAB250 surface analysis system. The hydrodynamic size distribution of CDs in water was determined using dynamic laser scattering (DLS) using ZetaPlus apparatus (Brookhaven Instruments, Holtsville, NY).

Fluorescent CDs were prepared by microwave irradiation assisted hydrothermal treatment. The experimental procedure was described in following section. Briefly, 500mg MPD and 100mg Glu were dissolved into 50mL ethylene glycol solution with shaking in the ultrasonic for 2min to dissolve sufficiently. And then the mixture was transferred into the 250mL triangular flask. It can be seen the mixed solution is in transparent state. The mixture was heated by microwave irradiation at 120°C on 240W for 5min by using microwave instruments (MCR-3). After that, the products were cooled down to room temperature naturally. And the color of solution was changed from transparent to dark red, suggesting the formation of MPD-Glu CDs. Then we put the mixed solution into the dialysis tubes (molecular weight cut off >3500Da) for 24h to remove the excess precursors and solvents. At the same time, the ethylene glycol molecules are also removed from the solution through the dialysis bag. More interesting, most of MPD-Glu CDs were still kept in the dialysis bag after removing the carbon residue because of their high water solubility. After dialysis, the solution of the dialysis bag was concentrated to about 2mL. And then it was dried under vacuum at 60°C for 24h. Finally, the purified MPD-Glu CDs can be obtained as black and round solid particles through the removal of water vapor by distillation. The process for synthesis of MPD-Glu CDs was displayed in Fig. 1 .

L929 cells were selected as the model cells to evaluate the biocompatibility of MPD-Glu CDs. In brief, L929 cells were cultured at 96 well plate by the density of 5×104 cellsmL−1, the Eagle's medium (DMEM; Gibco, Carlsbad, CA,) supplemented with bovine calf serum, cell incubator conditions under 5% carbon dioxide, 37°C constant temperature. The L929 cells were seeded at 96 well plate 24h, then each well was added with MPD-Glu CDs by different concentrations and went on culturing another 24h. After 24h, the L929 cells were used to start the cytotoxicity test. It must be emphasized that all experimental operations are carried out under aseptic conditions.

The cell uptake behavior of MPD-Glu CDs was conducted by the confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) Zeiss 710 3-channel (Zeiss, Germany) using L929 cells. The excitation wavelength was set as 405nm. L929 cells were cultured in a glass bottom dish with a density of 1×105 cells per dish. On the day of treatment, MPD-Glu CDs with a suitable concentration of 30μgmL−1 was incubated with cells for 3h at 37°C. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) to remove the uninternalized MPD-Glu CDs and then fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10min at room temperature.

The morphology and size of synthesized MPD-Glu CDs were investigated by TEM images. As shown in Fig. 2 , most of MPD-Glu CDs possess spherical shape with narrow size distribution between 15 and 35nm. The average size of MPD-Glu CDs is 22.33nm based on the TEM images. Moreover, the hydrodynamic size distribution of MPD-Glu CDs was also determined by DLS. The results demonstrated that the diameter of MPD-Glu CDs 166.48±72.62nm with polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.519 (Fig. S1). All of the above characterization results indicated that successful formation of spherical nanoparticles through reaction between MPD and Glu. The small size of MPD-Glu CDs is beneficial for their biomedical application. Previously, a number of reports have demonstrated that the fluorescent MPD-Glu CDs can be formed using small organic molecules under the hydrothermal treatment. In this procedure, these small organic molecules such as Glu, sodium citrate will be dehydrated and formation of nanostructures. Although the detailed mechanism is still under debate, the formation of fluorescent MPD-Glu CDs through these bottom-up methods is very useful and practical. Most of MPD-Glu CDs obtained from these reports are blue emission under UV light irradiation and the emission wavelengths are changed when using different excitation wavelengths. This provides a big challenge for the biological imaging and sensor applications of MPD-Glu CDs. Very recently, Lin et al. found that colorful CDs with red, green and blue luminescence could be prepared through hydrothermal treatment of aromatic amino compounds. This opens up a new door for the preparation of colorful CDs through change the raw materials [5]. However, most of these CDs are hydrophobic nature and poor dispersity in aqueous solution. Therefore the synthesis of high water dispersity CDs through the aromatic amino groups should be of great interest. And to the best of knowledge, the synthesis of fluorescent CDs through the hydrothermal treatment of Glu and aromatic amines has not been reported thus far.

To further evaluate the chemical bonds and functional groups on the surface of MPD-Glu CDs, these structures can be confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 , the absorption bands at 2956.4 and 1460.1cm−1 were simultaneously observed. This indicated the presence of aromatic hydrocarbons. Notably, the absorption band at 605nm implied the substitution of the phenyl ring meta group. In addition, the spectrum also shows the CN of benzene peak at 1378.9cm−1. The above results suggested that MPD was involved in the formation of MPD-Glu CDs. Moreover, the obvious peak emerged at 3405.8cm−1 and the band at 1623.8cm−1 should be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and Sp2 C, respectively. And the absorption peak at 2919.8cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of CH groups on the surface of MPD-Glu CDs. These function groups are derived from Glu. Therefore, we could conclude that the MPD-Glu CDs are successfully prepared from MPD and Glu through microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment.

The elemental analysis of the as prepared MPD-Glu CDs was further evaluated by XPS. As shown in Fig. 4 , the XPS spectra of the MPD-Glu CDs exhibited three typical C 1s peak at 285eV, N 1s peak at 406eV and O 1s peak at 535eV. These results indicate that the main elements of MPD-Glu CDs are carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. This implied that the MPD-Glu CDs are surely composited with MPD and Glu. Specifically, as Fig. 4B shows, the C 1s XPS spectrum has two peaks at 285.5 and 286.1eV, which are attributed to CC band and CN band, respectively. The deconvolutions of the O 1s spectrum are shown in Fig. 4D, the result reveal the carbon hydroxyl groups CO band at 532.0eV, and the OH band at 533.7eV. Moreover, because of the low nitrogen content, the characteristic peaks are not obvious (Fig. 4C). The corresponding element contents of C, N, and O are calculated to be about 75.42%, 0.43%, 24.15%, respectively. The appearance of N and O in MPD-Glu CDs clearly indicated that both MPD and Glu involved in the formation of CDs. Furthermore, the percentages of element contents from XPS also indicated that MPD-Glu CDs are mainly composited with Glu owing to the low content of N. In a word, the XPS analysis confirms that MPD-Glu CDs are composed with MPD and Glu simultaneously. This is the basic for the different optical properties of MPD-Glu CDs from other type of N-doped CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of MPD-Glu CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further investigate the optical properties of the as-synthesized MPD-Glu CDs, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum and the photoluminescence emission spectra of MPD-Glu CDs were characterized. Fig. 5 is the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of MPD-Glu CDs. Two typical peaks can be found. The absorption peak at 220nm can be ascribed to the π-π* transition of aromatic rings, while the shoulder peak at 296nm is a typical characteristic of benzene ring and arise come from the n-π* transition of electrons in the molecular orbital of CC in MPD-Glu CDs [53]. The inset images of Fig. 5 are the photographs of water suspension of MPD-Glu CDs under the UV light irradiation (left cuvette) and natural light (right cuvette). Uniform green fluorescence can observed when the water suspension was irradiation by UV lamp at λ=365nm. On the other hand, we also found that the water suspension of MPD-Glu CDs is brown color. The optical properties indicated that the successful formation of fluorescent CDs from MPD and Glu.

The fluorescence properties of MPD-Glu CDs were evaluated by fluorescence spectroscopy and results were displayed in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6A shows the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of MPD-Glu CDs. It can be seen that the maximum excitation and emission peaks are located at 370 and 500nm, respectively. Accompanying the changes of the excitation wavelengths from 330 to 390nm, the emission wavelengths were also red shift gradually and finally fixed at 500nm (Figs. 6B and S2). The maximum emission intensity was obtained when the excitation wavelength was 370nm. This is well consisted with the excitation spectrum of MPD-Glu CDs. The fluorescence quantum yield of MPD-Glu CDs was determined using quinine sulfate as the reference dye. Result suggests that the quantum yield is about 11.2%. This is a comparable with other fluorescent materials such as fluorescent polydopamine and starch based fluorescent polymers [54,55]. The fluorescence properties are obvious different many other CDs reported in previous reports [56–58]. The unique fluorescence properties of MPD-Glu CDs make them more suitable for biological imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH and illumination on CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The main application of CDs is in vitro, but the organism is a complex environment, the stability of MPD-Glu CDs is an indispensable condition in the intracellular environment. As Fig. 7A showed, the aqueous solution of the MPD-Glu CDs was continuously irradiated at intervals of 30min with the UV lamp by the excitation wavelength at 365nm, and the fluorescence intensity was not weakened. This fully demonstrated the stability of the MPD-Glu CDs. Meanwhile, pH was also of great concern to all life forms; the effect of pH on fluorescence intensity was tested. The Fig. 7B showed, the emission of the MPD-Glu CDs was weak under acidic conditions and increased from pH1 to pH6, in the neutral environment pH6 and pH7 having the strongest fluorescence intensity. Then, under the alkaline environment, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases with increasing from pH8 to pH12. This may be due to the gradual deprotonation of –NH+ 2 on the surface of the MPD-Glu CDs resulting in fluorescence enhanced when the pH changes from an acidic condition to a basic condition. These results indicated that the obtained MPD-Glu CDs were desirable pH probes to monitor the pH changes in vivo because of the sensitivity to pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and cell imaging of MPD-Glu CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent CDs have been widely used for biological imaging, chemical sensor and theranostics, owing to good water dispersibility, stable fluorescence and biocompatibility [59–65]. In order to evaluate the toxicity of MPD-Glu CDs, we used the L929 cell survival rate to determine toxicity of synthetic MPD-Glu CDs. The cell viability values of L929 cells were determined using different concentrations of MPD-Glu CDs ranging from 20 to100μgmL−1. The results were shown Fig. 8A, the data show that the cell viability values are gradually decreased with the increasing of concentrations of MPD-Glu CDs. However, the cell survival rate is still >80% when the concentration of MPD-Glu CDs was up to100μgmL−1. The above results implied that MPD-Glu CDs have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. In view of their potential biomedical applications, the cell uptake behavior must also be taken into account, the biological imaging experiments were carried out by the confocal fluorescence microscope with L929 cells. We found that the fluorescence signals could be observed in Fig. 8B. The fluorescence signals should be originated from the MPD-Glu CDs that were internalized by cells. Moreover, we also found that the L929 cells were firmly adhered to the cell plates very well and kept their normal morphology (Fig. 8C). This further indicated that MPD-Glu CDs possess low cytotoxicity. More importantly, the emerged image of Fig. 8B and C was displayed in Fig. 8D. From Fig. 8D, we can found that the locations with fluorescence signals are just covered with the cell locations. This clearly evidenced the successful uptaken of MPD-Glu CDs by L929 cells. It should be noted that the some areas with related weak fluorescence signals were observed from Fig. 8D. We speculated that these areas with weak fluorescence signals should be the locations of cell nuclei. It is therefore we can conclude that MPD-Glu CDs were mainly internalized by L929 cells through non-specific phagocytosis and mainly distributed in the cytoplasm. As reported, UV excitation is not good for biomedical applications because it could have damage for the biological tissues, while the visible range is between 400 and 700nm, the cell imaging was prepared by the excitation wavelength at 405nm. These results were sufficient to demonstrate the application of MPD-Glu CDs in biomedical applications [66,67]. Considering the high water dispersity, green emission, good biocompatibility and cell uptake behavior, the MPD-Glu CDs should be of great interest for biomedical applications.

In summary, we have developed a simple and environmentally friendly microwave-assisted method to prepare MPD-Glu CDs with green emission by using two cheap precursors as carbon sources. The MPD-Glu CDs can be obtained through microwave irradiation at 120°C within 5min under air atmosphere. The obtained MPD-Glu CDs show high luminescence, water dispersity, low cytotoxicity and cell imaging performance. As compared with previous methods, this work provides a novel strategy for fabrication of CDs with non-blue emission. More importantly, many other CDs with different emission properties and functions could also be fabricated through the similar procedure via choosing various precursors. Therefore, we believe that this work will open a new avenue for the synthesis and applications of CDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014, 51673107), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072, 20161BAB213066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.11.034.
